content:
Member-only story

15 min read
Just now

--

The LLM memory wall in real numbers: why inference is bandwidth-bound, what a KV cache costs at 128k context, and the five tricks that fight back.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

https://www.reactbd.com/Here’s a strange fact about July 2026: the scarcest thing in AI right now isn’t GPUs. It’s the memory around them. DRAM prices jumped roughly 90% in a single quarter, the big three memory makers are reported effectively sold out through the year, and Intel’s CEO says relief may not come before 2028 — all because AI data centers are eating the world’s memory supply. That’s the LLM memory wall arriving in your electronics bill, and it’s the visible symptom of something most engineers still get backwards: serving a large language model is not primarily a compute problem. It’s a memory problem — capacity and bandwidth — and it has been for a while.
In this post I’ll show you the whole thing with numbers you can check: why a top-end GPU spends most of a chat generation waiting rather than computing, what the KV cache actually is (with the one analogy that finally made it click for me), and a 12-line Python calculator — which I ran against the real config files of Llama and Qwen models — that tells you exactly how many gigabytes one long conversation costs. Then the five levers that fight the wall, why a Mac on your desk is quietly one of the best inference…
